results.possibleIdentity:
Alan Bennett Shalleck is a technical consultant, engineer, and entrepreneur who was active in the fields of computer design and information processing during the late 1960s and early 1970s. A 1960 graduate of the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT), Shalleck worked as a consultant for The Diebold Group, Inc. in New York City, where he focused on computer design and information processing systems. During this period, he was involved in professional discussions regarding data procedures and hardware evaluation, notably serving as a moderator for technical sessions on computer design. He is also identified as a member of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), a non-profit organization that promoted collaborations between the technical and artistic communities.

Following his tenure at Diebold, Shalleck transitioned into entrepreneurship. He founded Princeton Laboratories, a company specializing in immunological diagnostics that developed the first over-the-counter pregnancy test; the business was sold to Carter-Wallace in 1974. He subsequently established Aegis Systems Corporation, a microprocessor-based firm focused on energy conservation technology, and later led ventures such as Lifestyle 2000 and TEKNET, Inc. In his later career, he served as a visiting lecturer on entrepreneurism at Princeton University and as the president of NanoClarity LLC, a nanotechnology consultancy. He is the son of the late New York judge Milton Shalleck.

He is also identified as a member of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), a non-profit organization that promoted collaborations between the technical and artistic communities.\n\nFollowing his tenure at Diebold, Shalleck transitioned into entrepreneurship.
He founded Princeton Laboratories, a company specializing in immunological diagnostics that developed the first over-the-counter pregnancy test; the business was sold to Carter-Wallace in 1974.
He subsequently established Aegis Systems Corporation, a microprocessor-based firm focused on energy conservation technology, and later led ventures such as Lifestyle 2000 and TEKNET, Inc.
In his later career, he served as a visiting lecturer on entrepreneurism at Princeton University and as the president of NanoClarity LLC, a nanotechnology consultancy.
